title: Private Internet Access (PIA) VPN Review 2026: Court-Tested No-Logs, US Jurisdiction, and the $2/Month Reality Check
metaDescription: Our honest Private Internet Access review for April 2026. We examined Kape Technologies ownership, US jurisdiction vs the 2016/2017 court cases, Deloitte audits, port forwarding, open-source apps, current pricing, streaming performance, and how PIA compares to NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad.
pros: No-logs claim tested in at least two publicly documented US court cases (2016 FBI grand jury, 2017 civil case) where PIA was legally compelled to produce user data and had none to hand over
Independently audited twice by Deloitte Romania (2022 and 2024) covering server configuration and no-logs policy
Fully open-source client applications on Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android — source code published on github.com/pia-foss for independent verification
Port forwarding still supported on select servers — one of the few major consumer VPNs that still offers it (NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad have all removed it)
Unlimited simultaneous device connections on a single account — no device cap, no router workaround required
Large advertised server network (the PIA site cites 35,000+ servers across 91 countries, including servers in all 50 US states)
PIA MACE — built-in DNS-level ad, tracker, and malware blocker that works across all apps and browsers, not just inside a browser extension
Introductory pricing among the cheapest in the mainstream VPN market (€1.75/month on the current 3-year promo, with a 30-day money-back guarantee)
cons: Based in the United States — a founding member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance, which is a legitimate structural privacy concern regardless of how strong the no-logs execution is
Owned by Kape Technologies, the same holding company that owns ExpressVPN, CyberGhost, and ZenMate — a level of portfolio concentration that privacy-maximalists reasonably push back on

Speeds are mid-tier, not top-tier — independent reviewers (Security.org, CyberInsider, vpnMentor) consistently place PIA behind NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark on long-distance routes, especially on upload
Streaming unblocking requires manually selecting dedicated streaming servers — regular PIA servers frequently get blocked by Netflix, and the experience is less seamless than NordVPN's SmartPlay
Introductory rate is a loss leader — the 3-year plan advertised at €1.75/month autorenews at a substantially higher annual bill, which is worth flagging before you subscribe
The desktop app interface is more cluttered than NordVPN's or ExpressVPN's — power users love the granularity, first-time VPN buyers can find it overwhelming
The 35,000+ servers claim is a marketing headline that mixes physical and virtual infrastructure — the practically useful count is lower and is not broken out cleanly on the PIA site

testResults.downloadSpeed: Independent 2026 tests from Security.org, CyberInsider, and vpnMentor place PIA WireGuard download speeds in the 150–220 Mbps range on nearby servers from mid-tier baselines, with Security.org noting that download retention on nearby servers is very close to baseline (approximately 4–5% loss) but drops significantly on long-distance routes. Do not expect NordLynx-class peak numbers.
testResults.uploadSpeed: Upload is PIA's weakest real-world metric. Security.org measured upload-speed drops of approximately 85% versus the unprotected baseline on long-distance servers — a consistent pattern also reported by CyberInsider and vpnMentor. Nearby servers retain more upload, but heavy-upload workloads (cloud backup, content uploads) are where you feel the gap versus NordVPN and Surfshark.
testResults.latency: Sub-15 ms on nearby servers in WireGuard mode; 100–230 ms on transatlantic and transpacific routes in independent 2026 tests.
testResults.speedRetention: Retention on nearby servers is strong in independent 2026 tests; long-distance retention tails off more than NordVPN or ExpressVPN, particularly on upload. Expect excellent nearby-server performance and mid-tier long-distance performance.
testResults.noLogsClaim: Verified twice by Deloitte Romania (2022 and 2024) covering server configuration and no-logs policy. Additionally, PIA's no-logs claim was tested in at least two publicly documented US court cases — a 2016 FBI grand jury subpoena (first reported by TorrentFreak, citing Almanac News coverage of the Arsenault testimony) and a 2017 civil case — in both of which PIA was legally compelled to produce user data and produced none.

plans.name: 3 Years
plans.discount: Best Value — headline promo rate on the PIA checkout page (April 2026)
extras: Unlimited simultaneous device connections on a single account
All VPN protocols included — WireGuard, OpenVPN (UDP/TCP), IKEv2/IPsec on iOS
Port forwarding available on select servers
Open-source client apps on every platform
Autorenews annually after the initial 36-month term at a substantially higher rate — set a calendar reminder

plans.name: 1 Year
extras: Autorenews annually at the same 12-month bill

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: 30 days on all plans, including the monthly plan.
pricing.freeTrial: No desktop free trial. 7-day free trial available on Android and iOS through the respective app stores. The 30-day money-back guarantee on paid plans functions as an extended risk-free window for desktop users.

reviewContent:
## Private Internet Access Review 2026: Court-Tested No-Logs in a US Jurisdiction Private Internet Access — universally called PIA — is one of the oldest consumer VPNs in the market, dating back to 2010. It is also one of the most ideologically complicated to review in 2026, because three of the most important things about PIA cut in different directions. It is **based in the United States**, a founding member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance. That is the single biggest structural knock against PIA and you will see it in the first paragraph of almost every competitor's review. It is also one of the only mainstream VPNs whose **no-logs claim has been tested in open court**, by the US government, in at least two publicly documented cases (2016 and 2017). In both cases, PIA was subpoenaed for user data and had none to produce. That is a real, evidence-based rebuttal to the US-jurisdiction concern — not marketing. And it is owned by **Kape Technologies**, the same holding company that owns ExpressVPN, CyberGhost, and ZenMate — a level of portfolio concentration that privacy-maximalists push back on for reasons we cover below. PIA is the third Kape-owned VPN we have reviewed in depth on this site, after ExpressVPN and CyberGhost. This review is written to give you the real picture in April 2026 — what PIA actually does well, where it genuinely underperforms, how to interpret the US-jurisdiction and Kape-ownership questions honestly, and whether the famous $2/month (or in the European pricing we saw on the checkout page, €1.75/month) headline price is as good as it sounds. For broader context, see our best VPN rankings for 2026, our best no-logs VPN picks, and our best cheap VPN guide. ### Our Verdict PIA earns a 4.2/5 from us in April 2026. The server network is genuinely large, the open-source commitment is real (source code on github.com/pia-foss), port forwarding is still supported on select servers (a feature NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Mullvad have all removed), unlimited device connections on one account is a practical win for large households, and the court-tested no-logs story is the most evidence-heavy in the industry. Where PIA loses points: US jurisdiction is a legitimate structural concern for privacy-maximalists regardless of how strong the no-logs execution is; speeds are mid-tier rather than top-tier on long-distance servers; streaming requires you to manually pick dedicated streaming servers rather than working automatically everywhere; and the introductory €1.75/month rate is a loss leader that jumps substantially on renewal. **Best for:** torrent users who need port forwarding, large households that want unlimited device connections, Linux users who want a full-featured GUI app, power users who want granular control over encryption and protocol settings, and budget-conscious privacy buyers who are comfortable with a US-based provider whose no-logs policy has been proven in court. **Not best for:** privacy-maximalists who categorically avoid US jurisdictions and Kape-owned services (go to Mullvad or IVPN), speed-first users who want top-tier long-distance throughput (go to NordVPN), or first-time VPN buyers who want the most polished one-click experience (go to ExpressVPN). --- ## Speed: Mid-Tier on Long-Distance, Solid on Nearby Independent 2026 speed tests of PIA paint a consistent picture across Security.org, CyberInsider, vpnMentor, and WizCase: PIA is fast enough for anything a normal user does on a nearby server, but it cannot match NordVPN or ExpressVPN once you start connecting across continents. What recent independent reviewers measured on WireGuard, the default protocol: | Source | Setup | Headline result | |---|---|---| | Security.org (2026) | Multi-region WireGuard tests | Nearby-server download retention very close to baseline (~4–5% loss); upload drops of roughly 85% on long-distance routes | | CyberInsider (2026) | WireGuard, US baseline | Solid nearby performance; transatlantic and transpacific routes noticeably slower than NordLynx | | vpnMentor (2026) | Mixed distance WireGuard tests | Decent nearby-server numbers; consistent ranking below NordVPN and ExpressVPN on raw throughput | | WizCase (2026) | PIA WireGuard vs baseline | "Fast enough for HD/4K streaming and gaming" — below top-tier on long-distance | Three honest takeaways: 1. **On nearby servers, PIA is fine for anything a normal user does.** HD and 4K streaming, video calls, gaming, general browsing, and moderate file downloads are not bottlenecked. On a 250–1000 Mbps home connection, you will not notice the gap between PIA and NordVPN in day-to-day use on a nearby server. 2. **Upload is PIA's consistent weak spot.** Security.org's finding that upload drops by roughly 85% versus the unprotected baseline on long-distance servers is echoed by other testers. If you upload a lot — cloud backup, content creation, streaming out rather than in — the gap versus NordVPN/Surfshark becomes material rather than theoretical. 3. **WireGuard is the right default.** Historical PIA reviews sometimes showed OpenVPN UDP/TCP numbers that were embarrassingly slow. WireGuard, which is the modern default on all PIA apps, closes most of that gap. Only switch off WireGuard if you have a specific compatibility reason.
💡 Pro Tip: If PIA feels slow, check that you are on WireGuard (Settings → Protocols → WireGuard) rather than OpenVPN. On the desktop app, you can also drop the encryption from AES-256 to AES-128 on OpenVPN connections without a practically meaningful security reduction for most threat models — but WireGuard is almost always the better option anyway.
--- ## Security and Privacy: The Core Argument for PIA This is the section that matters most. PIA's sales pitch — and frankly the reason to choose it — lives and dies here. ### Jurisdiction: United States (the Honest Version) PIA is operated by Private Internet Access, Inc., a Kape Technologies subsidiary headquartered in Denver, Colorado. The United States is a founding member of the Five Eyes intelligence alliance, and US authorities can legally compel US-based companies to hand over user data through various legal processes (subpoenas, National Security Letters with gag orders, grand jury proceedings). That is a real structural concern and we are not going to hand-wave it. Here is what materially offsets it — and this is where PIA is almost unique: **1. No US data retention law for VPNs.** Unlike some European countries that have historically required telecoms-style logging, the US has no federal law compelling a VPN provider to retain user activity logs. The legal environment does not *force* logging. A US-based VPN can legitimately operate a no-logs network; what matters is whether you believe the provider's execution. **2. The 2016 FBI subpoena.** In a widely reported 2016 case, PIA was issued a federal grand jury subpoena in connection with an FBI investigation. TorrentFreak reported the story at the time, citing Almanac News coverage of FBI special agent Jeffrey Arsenault's testimony, which specifically acknowledged that "some VPN companies, PIA included, do not retain logs of customers' Internet activities." PIA produced no user data, because none existed. This is on public record and has been cited in subsequent coverage by TechRadar, PCWorld, and TorrentFreak. **3. The 2017 civil case.** A second publicly documented case in 2017, in which PIA was again legally compelled to produce user data and again had none to hand over. This case is discussed in subsequent reviews and in PIA's own privacy-audit transparency materials. **4. Deloitte Romania no-logs audits in 2022 and 2024.** Two separate assurance engagements in which Deloitte — one of the Big Four accounting firms — examined PIA's server infrastructure and IT management systems and confirmed that the architecture does not collect or store user activity data. The audit reports are published on PIA's website. The honest synthesis: PIA's jurisdiction is objectively the worst of the mainstream privacy-focused VPNs. Its execution track record is among the best in the industry — arguably the most battle-tested in open court. Whether that trade is acceptable depends on your threat model. For most normal consumers, it is. For journalists, activists, or anyone with a targeted-surveillance concern, a provider outside Five Eyes with equivalent technical controls (Mullvad in Sweden, ProtonVPN in Switzerland, NordVPN in Panama) is the safer default. ### Ownership: Kape Technologies PIA was acquired by Kape Technologies (then Crossrider) in November 2019. Kape's VPN portfolio now includes ExpressVPN (December 2021, ~$936m), CyberGhost (March 2017), and ZenMate (2018), plus Intego macOS antivirus. Two honest observations: 1. **The Crossrider-era adware history is real but is not PIA's history** — it is Crossrider's platform that was abused pre-rebrand, pre-acquisition. Under Kape, PIA has completed two Deloitte no-logs audits (2022 and 2024), continued its court-tested no-logs execution, and kept its client apps fully open source. 2. **The portfolio concentration is a structural concern even if PIA itself is clean.** One company owning ExpressVPN + CyberGhost + PIA + ZenMate means a single bad policy decision or future acquisition could propagate across all four. Privacy-maximalists argue for diversifying across independently-owned providers (Mullvad, IVPN, ProtonVPN) as a structural hedge. Reasonable position. Our take: if you are a normal consumer, Kape ownership is not materially disqualifying. If you have a targeted threat model, read the Wikipedia entry on Kape Technologies yourself and make your own call. ### Encryption, Protocols, and Network Architecture - **AES-256 or AES-128** on OpenVPN connections — user-selectable, unusual granularity for a consumer VPN. - **ChaCha20** on WireGuard — the modern default. - **WireGuard** available on every platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android) and the recommended default. - **OpenVPN** (UDP and TCP) available with detailed tunables for users who want them. - **IKEv2/IPsec** on iOS as a supplementary option. - **Shadowsocks** proxy for obfuscating VPN traffic in restrictive networks. - **RAM-only servers** — PIA's server fleet runs from RAM rather than disk, which means a reboot or power cycle wipes all runtime state. Combined with the no-logs policy, this means even a physical server seizure would yield no persistent user data. - **Private DNS** — PIA operates its own DNS resolvers. Across DNS leak tests at dnsleaktest.com and ipleak.net, we saw no leaks. - **Kill switch** — three modes (Off / Auto / Always). The Always mode blocks all internet traffic unless the VPN is connected, even across reboots. - **Split tunneling** available on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android (not iOS, per Apple's network-extension constraints). - **Multi-hop** — PIA's multi-hop routes your traffic through a VPN server and then through a SOCKS5 or Shadowsocks proxy, rather than through two VPN servers chained like NordVPN's Double VPN. Different model, similar end result. ### Open-Source Clients PIA is one of the very few mainstream consumer VPNs where every client application is fully open source. The source code for the Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android apps is published on **github.com/pia-foss** for independent review. That means security researchers, journalists, and technically inclined users can verify what the software actually does, rather than trusting a closed binary. This matters because a VPN client is the most privileged piece of networking software on your device — it sees your full unencrypted traffic before routing it through the tunnel. If the client were quietly telemetering user behaviour or leaking data to a third party, an open-source codebase would expose that. Closed-source VPN clients (the vast majority) require you to take the provider's word for it. ### PIA MACE PIA MACE is a built-in DNS-level ad, tracker, and malware blocker. Because it works at the DNS layer, it filters requests across every app on your device — not just inside a browser, the way an extension like uBlock Origin does. In practice, MACE blocks a large majority of ad and tracker requests without a noticeable performance hit.
💡 Pro Tip: Enable PIA MACE in Settings → Privacy to get DNS-level ad, tracker, and malware blocking across every app on your device. Unlike browser extensions, MACE works inside mobile apps, smart-TV apps, and command-line tools where extensions cannot reach. For most users, MACE combined with uBlock Origin in the browser covers the overwhelming majority of ads and trackers you will encounter.
--- ## Streaming Performance Streaming is where PIA's honest framing matters. The service works with major streaming platforms — but only when you use the dedicated streaming-optimized servers, which you must select manually. Regular PIA servers get detected and blocked by Netflix frequently enough that we cannot recommend them for streaming. In April 2026 testing across independent reviewers and our own checks on dedicated streaming servers: | Platform | Result | Notes | |---|---|---| | Netflix US | Working | US streaming-optimized server required; regular US servers often blocked | | Netflix UK | Working | UK streaming server required | | Netflix Japan | Working | Japan streaming server required | | BBC iPlayer | Working | UK streaming server worked consistently for HD playback | | Disney+ | Working | US streaming server worked on first attempt | | Amazon Prime Video (US) | Working | US library accessible via streaming-optimized servers | | Hulu | Working | Streaming server worked; Hulu remains one of the more aggressive VPN-detection platforms | | HBO Max (Max) | Working | US streaming server worked in our April 2026 checks | | Paramount+ | Working | Dedicated streaming server required | | DAZN, Peacock | Partial | Worked on some servers, blocked on others — expect to rotate | Independent reviewers are split on the severity of the caveat. PIA's own landing page advertises 10+ streaming-optimized countries; 01net's review notes that PIA "unblocks merely 5–6 catalogues" in its testing; Gizmodo reports "up to seven libraries." Our own view, consistent with Security.org and CyberInsider, is that **PIA works with major streaming platforms when you use the streaming-optimized server list, and does not work reliably if you connect to a generic server and hope for the best.** NordVPN's SmartPlay DNS and CyberGhost's per-service optimized-server lists are both smoother user experiences for streaming. If streaming is your primary use case, those are the better picks. If streaming is one of several use cases and you are mostly watching major US/UK/JP libraries, PIA is fine — you just need to select the right server. For devices that cannot run the PIA app directly (Apple TV, some smart TVs, consoles), PIA offers Smart DNS configuration and router-level deployment as alternatives. --- ## Pricing and the €1.75/Month Reality Check On the PIA buy-vpn-online checkout page (captured April 19, 2026, from a European IP), the headline prices are in EUR: | Plan | Per month | Total bill | Renewal | |---|---|---|---| | 3 Years | €1.75 | Introductory-priced 36-month plan | Autorenews annually after the 36-month term at a substantially higher rate | | 1 Year | €3.10 | €37.20 for 12 months | Autorenews at the 12-month rate | | 1 Month | €11.69 | €11.69/month | Autorenews monthly | USD-displaying promotions on the main US homepage commonly show equivalent rates in the $2.03–$2.19/month range on the 3-year plan, with Cybernews citing $2.03/month in its April 2026 comparison coverage. The exact currency and exact per-month rate depends on your billing country, promotional window, and campaign — PIA runs rolling campaigns — but the pattern is consistent: the 3-year plan is the loss-leader tier, and is meaningfully cheaper per month than the 1-year or monthly plans. **What is actually cheap, and what is not:** The €1.75/month (≈ $2/month USD) headline is the introductory rate on the 3-year plan. It is a real price for the first 36 months; it is not the renewal rate. After the 36-month term ends, the subscription autorenews annually at a substantially higher bill. PIA's pricing page does not prominently surface the renewal rate during the initial purchase flow, which is an industry-standard UX pattern across NordVPN, CyberGhost, Surfshark, and ExpressVPN — but is nonetheless worth flagging before you subscribe. If you plan to keep PIA for the long term, you have three practical options: 1. Keep the subscription and pay the renewal rate once the 36-month term ends. 2. Set a calendar reminder 10 days before the renewal date and contact support for a retention discount (this works more often than users expect). 3. Cancel and re-sign up under a fresh email / payment combo to lock in the intro rate for another 36 months — a common practice in the VPN subscription market that most users learn after their first renewal surprise. **Money-back guarantee:** 30 days on every plan, including the monthly plan. This is the industry-standard window, simpler than CyberGhost's split 45-day-on-annual / 14-day-on-monthly structure. No-questions-asked in practice, based on independent reviewer reports and our own spot checks. **Payment methods:** Credit and debit cards, PayPal, Amazon Pay, and a range of cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin, and others) through PIA's payment processor — useful for users who prefer an anonymous signup flow. **Add-ons:** Dedicated IP (static IP in 10+ countries, useful for accessing IP-whitelisted services and avoiding repeated CAPTCHA challenges) and PIA Antivirus (Windows and Android bundle) are available as paid add-ons at checkout rather than bundled into higher plan tiers. The overall plan structure is simpler than NordVPN's Basic/Plus/Complete/Prime ladder — one PIA plan, all features included, pay extra only if you want a dedicated IP or antivirus.
💡 Pro Tip: If you want to test PIA without commitment, the smart move is to buy the 3-year plan (not the monthly). The €1.75/month intro rate locks in for 36 months if you keep it, and the 30-day money-back guarantee gives you a full 30-day no-questions-asked test window. Buying the monthly plan for a "trial" costs you €11.69 at full rate for the same 30-day refund window.
--- ## Server Network: The 35,000+ Claim, Honestly PIA's marketing prominently cites "over 35,000 servers in 91 countries." Independent reviewers cite lower numbers — Security.org cited roughly 30,000 earlier in the 2025–2026 cycle, CyberInsider has cited similar numbers, and the older 2026 reviews sometimes cite 29,650. There are two honest things to say about this: 1. **The number is not fully broken out into physical vs virtual servers** on the PIA site. This is an industry-wide issue and is not unique to PIA. What you can verify is the country count (91) and the presence of servers in all 50 US states and across major population centres in Europe, Asia, Latin America, and Oceania. 2. **For practical use, 35,000 vs 10,000 is a distinction without a difference.** What matters is whether there is a working server with good latency in each country you actually want to connect to. On that metric, PIA's coverage is genuinely excellent — 91 countries including less-covered markets (all 50 US states is a differentiator, because some services geofence at the state level within the US). **Specialty servers:** - **Streaming-optimized servers** — available in 10+ countries for Netflix US/UK/JP/CA/AU, BBC iPlayer, Disney+, and others. - **Port-forwarding-enabled servers** — select locations, a genuine differentiator (see below). - **Dedicated IP** — optional add-on from 10+ country options. - **Multi-hop (VPN + SOCKS5/Shadowsocks)** — for users who want a second layer of obfuscation. ### Port Forwarding: A Genuine Differentiator Port forwarding enables inbound connections to a specific port inside the VPN tunnel. For torrent users, this is critical — without port forwarding, your BitTorrent client can only initiate outbound connections, which meaningfully limits peer count, download speed, and seeding ratio. NordVPN removed port forwarding in 2024. ExpressVPN has not supported it for years. Mullvad removed it in 2023 due to abuse concerns. That leaves PIA as one of the very few mainstream consumer VPNs that still offers port forwarding on select servers in 2026. If port forwarding is a requirement for your workflow (private trackers, self-hosted services, seedbox-adjacent use), PIA is effectively the default mainstream option at this point. --- ## Apps and Usability Native PIA apps are available for Windows, macOS, Linux (full GUI), iOS, Android, Android TV, and Amazon Fire TV. There are browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Opera — note, as with CyberGhost, that a VPN browser extension is a proxy-only tool, not a full-device tunnel; if you care about full device protection, use the desktop app rather than the extension alone. Router support includes DD-WRT, Tomato, pfSense, and OpenWrt through configuration files and setup guides, plus Smart DNS for consoles and smart TVs that cannot run a VPN client directly. **Linux experience:** PIA is one of the strongest Linux VPN clients in the consumer market. The Linux app is a full GUI (not a CLI-only wrapper) with feature parity to the Windows and macOS apps — kill switch, split tunneling, MACE, protocol selection, and port forwarding all available through the interface. Mullvad and ProtonVPN are the other strong Linux choices; PIA is squarely in that top tier. **Device limit:** Unlimited. This is a practical advantage for households with lots of devices, shared accounts within a family, and users who also want their phone, laptop, tablet, desktop, smart TV, and a handful of IoT devices protected without hitting a device cap. NordVPN caps at 10, ExpressVPN at 8; Surfshark also offers unlimited. PIA's unlimited-devices policy is a genuine feature. **Interface:** PIA's desktop app uses a compact tray-expandable panel with a clean quick-connect button and an extensive settings drawer. The settings drawer is where PIA's character shows — you can tune encryption level, protocol, DNS resolver, kill switch behaviour, split tunnel rules, MACE, port forwarding, and multi-hop independently. Power users love this granularity; newcomers can find it overwhelming. If you want the simplest possible one-click experience, ExpressVPN's app is still the cleanest in the category. --- ## How PIA Compares | Metric | PIA | NordVPN | ExpressVPN | Mullvad | |---|---|---|---|---| | Lowest monthly (long plan) | €1.75 (3-year intro) | $3.39 (2-year) | $3.49 (2-year) | €5 flat (no tiers) | | Simultaneous devices | Unlimited | 10 | 8 | 5 | | Jurisdiction | United States (Five Eyes) | Panama | British Virgin Islands | Sweden (14 Eyes, but strong privacy law) | | Parent company | Kape Technologies | Nord Security | Kape Technologies | Mullvad AB (independent) | | No-logs audits | Deloitte × 2 (2022, 2024) + court-tested in 2016/2017 | Deloitte × 6 | KPMG, PwC, Cure53 (20+ total audits) | Assured AB audit (2023) | | Port forwarding | Yes (select servers) | No (removed 2024) | No | No (removed 2023) | | Open-source clients | Yes (all platforms, github.com/pia-foss) | No (NordLynx protocol is open; apps are not fully open) | No (Lightway protocol is open; apps are not) | Yes (all apps open source) | | Signup privacy | Email required, crypto payment option | Email required | Email required | Fully anonymous (account number only, cash accepted by mail) | | Money-back guarantee | 30 days (all plans) | 30 days | 30 days | 30 days | ### PIA vs NordVPN The comparison most PIA shoppers actually make. NordVPN wins on audit cadence (six Deloitte engagements to PIA's two), on raw speed (NordLynx is consistently faster in independent benchmarks — CyberInsider measured 903 Mbps peak on NordVPN versus mid-tier speeds on PIA), on streaming smoothness (SmartPlay DNS unblocks major platforms automatically without manual streaming-server selection), and on jurisdiction (Panama is outside the 14-Eyes, unlike the US). PIA wins on price at the introductory rate, on unlimited simultaneous devices (NordVPN caps at 10), on port forwarding (NordVPN removed it in 2024), on the court-tested-in-open-court evidence of its no-logs claim (NordVPN has not been subject to a comparable public court test), and on fully open-source client apps. For most users who prioritise top-tier speed and streaming smoothness, NordVPN is worth the price premium. For users who prioritise unlimited devices, port forwarding, or open-source verifiability, PIA is the right answer even after accounting for the jurisdiction concern. See our PIA vs NordVPN head-to-head for the full breakdown. ### PIA vs ExpressVPN (Same Kape Parent) These are both Kape-owned, which makes the comparison a useful internal one. ExpressVPN is the premium-positioned Kape brand: more audits, faster Lightway protocol, better streaming unblocking, cleaner one-click UX, higher price point. PIA is the value-positioned Kape brand: cheaper intro price, unlimited devices (vs 8 on ExpressVPN), port forwarding still supported (ExpressVPN does not offer it), fully open-source clients (ExpressVPN publishes Lightway but not the full apps), Linux GUI parity. If you are specifically comparing these two and the Kape ownership is not a deal-breaker, the choice is between paying more for ExpressVPN's polish and speed versus saving money with PIA's feature-rich lower-tier product. Both are backed by the same parent. ### PIA vs Mullvad (Privacy-Maximalist Alternative) Mullvad is the correct comparison if you are specifically uncomfortable with US jurisdiction or Kape ownership. Mullvad is Swedish, independently owned, and one of the very few VPNs with a fully anonymous signup flow (account number only — no email required, cash mailed in an envelope accepted). Mullvad's flat €5/month pricing with no tiers and no long-term lock-in is structurally different from PIA's promotional-pricing model. PIA wins on price (€1.75/month intro vs €5/month flat), on unlimited devices (Mullvad caps at 5), on port forwarding (Mullvad removed it in 2023), and on server network size. Mullvad wins on jurisdiction, on signup privacy, and on simplicity (one plan, one price, no promotional timers). If you are a privacy-maximalist first and a price-shopper second, Mullvad. If you are a price-shopper first and a privacy-concerned user second, PIA. --- ## Who Should Choose PIA PIA is the right pick if: - You need port forwarding for torrenting, private trackers, or self-hosted services - You want unlimited simultaneous device connections without a router workaround - You are a Linux user who wants a full-featured GUI app (not a CLI-only experience) - You are a power user who wants granular control over encryption, protocols, DNS, kill switch, and split tunnel rules - You value open-source verifiability of the client apps (code on github.com/pia-foss) - You are comfortable with a US-based provider whose no-logs execution has been tested in open court - You want premium VPN features at the lowest competitive introductory price point in the mainstream market PIA is not the right pick if: - You categorically avoid US jurisdictions for your VPN (go to Mullvad, ProtonVPN, or NordVPN) - You categorically avoid Kape-owned VPNs (go to Mullvad, IVPN, or ProtonVPN) - You need top-tier long-distance speeds or heavy upload throughput (NordVPN, ExpressVPN) - You want the smoothest possible streaming UX with automatic server selection (NordVPN's SmartPlay) - You want anonymous signup with no email or payment link (Mullvad, IVPN) - You are a first-time VPN buyer who wants the simplest one-click app (ExpressVPN) --- ## Frequently Asked Questions **Is Private Internet Access safe to use in 2026?** Yes, in the practical sense. PIA uses AES-256 and ChaCha20 encryption, runs a RAM-only server architecture, operates its own private DNS, and has had its no-logs policy independently audited by Deloitte in 2022 and 2024. More importantly, PIA's no-logs claim has been tested in at least two publicly documented US court cases (2016 FBI grand jury subpoena, 2017 civil case) in which PIA was legally compelled to produce user data and had none to hand over. The remaining concern is structural: PIA is US-based, which is a Five Eyes jurisdiction. Whether that is disqualifying depends entirely on your threat model. **Is PIA still trustworthy given Kape Technologies ownership?** Legitimate question. Kape's predecessor Crossrider had historical adware-distribution associations pre-rebrand, but PIA as a VPN service has not been implicated in any post-acquisition scandal. Under Kape, PIA has completed two Deloitte audits (2022, 2024), kept apps open source on github.com/pia-foss, and continued court-tested no-logs execution. For normal consumers, not materially disqualifying. For privacy-maximalists, Mullvad, ProtonVPN, and IVPN are non-Kape alternatives. **Which VPNs does Kape Technologies own?** As of 2026: Private Internet Access (November 2019), CyberGhost (March 2017), ZenMate (2018), and ExpressVPN (December 2021, for approximately $936 million). Kape also owns Intego macOS antivirus. **What is the PIA 2016 court case?** In 2016, PIA was issued a US federal grand jury subpoena in connection with an FBI investigation. FBI special agent Jeffrey Arsenault's testimony — first reported by TorrentFreak, citing Almanac News coverage — specifically acknowledged that "some VPN companies, PIA included, do not retain logs of customers' Internet activities." PIA was legally compelled to produce user data and produced none. A second publicly documented case in 2017 produced the same result. **Does PIA work with Netflix in 2026?** Yes, on dedicated streaming-optimized servers. In April 2026 testing, PIA unblocked Netflix US, Netflix UK, and Netflix Japan via its streaming-labelled servers. Regular PIA servers are frequently blocked by Netflix's VPN detection, so you do need to manually select a streaming server. For a more automatic streaming experience, NordVPN's SmartPlay DNS and CyberGhost's per-service optimized lists are smoother user experiences. **How many devices can I connect to PIA at once?** Unlimited. There is no device cap on a PIA account — you can connect your phone, laptop, desktop, tablet, smart TV, router, and every other device you own simultaneously. This is one of PIA's clearest practical advantages; NordVPN caps at 10 and ExpressVPN caps at 8. **Does PIA support port forwarding?** Yes, on select servers. PIA is one of the very few mainstream consumer VPNs that still supports port forwarding in 2026. NordVPN removed port forwarding in 2024, ExpressVPN has not supported it for years, and Mullvad removed it in 2023 due to abuse concerns. Port forwarding is a practical requirement for some torrent workflows (private trackers, seeding ratios) and for a few self-hosted use cases. **Is PIA faster than NordVPN?** No — NordVPN is faster in independent 2026 benchmarks. CyberInsider measured a 903 Mbps peak on NordVPN via NordLynx, versus mid-tier results on PIA WireGuard. The raw-throughput gap is most visible on long-distance servers and on upload-heavy workloads. For nearby-server HD/4K streaming, browsing, and video calls, PIA is fast enough that the gap is not practically noticeable. **How much does PIA cost in 2026?** On the PIA buy-vpn-online checkout page in April 2026 (European IP), the rates are €1.75/month on the 3-year plan, €3.10/month on the 1-year plan, and €11.69/month on the monthly plan. USD rates are in the $2.03–$2.19/month range on the 3-year plan depending on the current promo. The 3-year introductory rate is a loss leader; the subscription autorenews annually at a substantially higher rate once the initial 36-month term ends. **What is PIA MACE?** PIA MACE is a built-in DNS-level ad, tracker, and malware blocker included in all PIA apps. Because it works at the DNS layer, it filters requests across every app on your device — not just inside a browser, the way an ad-blocker extension does. This is a meaningful advantage over pure browser-extension blockers, because it catches tracking and ads inside mobile apps, smart-TV apps, and command-line tools where extensions cannot reach. **Is PIA open source?** Yes, fully. Every PIA client application — Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android — has its source code published on github.com/pia-foss for independent review. This is unusual among mainstream consumer VPNs; NordVPN publishes its NordLynx protocol layer as open source but does not publish the full apps, and ExpressVPN publishes Lightway but not the full apps. Fully open-source clients are one of PIA's clearest structural advantages for users who care about verifiability rather than trust. **Can I use PIA in China or other restrictive countries?** PIA offers Shadowsocks-based obfuscation that can help in some restrictive networks, but — like every other VPN in the market — the experience in China specifically is cat-and-mouse and not guaranteed. If your use case includes regular travel to restrictive networks, test before arrival and have a backup provider. NordVPN's NordWhisper protocol (disguising VPN traffic as HTTPS) and ExpressVPN's Lightway with obfuscation are somewhat more sophisticated on this specific axis. --- ## How PIA Compares Wondering how PIA stacks up against the competition? Check out our head-to-head comparisons:
  • PIA vs NordVPN — the speed, audit, and jurisdiction trade-off
  • ExpressVPN Review — the premium-positioned Kape sibling
  • Mullvad VPN Review — the privacy-maximalist, non-Kape alternative
  • CyberGhost Review — the other value-positioned Kape-owned VPN
